source_intake_record.raw_question_text: The betatron accelerates particles through the emf induced by an increasing magnetic field within the particle's orbit. Let $\bar{B}_{1}$ be the average field within the particle orbit of radius $R$, and let $B_{2}$ be the field at the orbit (see Fig. 2.80). What must be the relationship between $\bar{B}_{1}$ and $B_{2}$ if the particle is to remain in the orbit at radius $R$ independent of its energy?

The phrase 'independent of its energy' adds an extra constraint beyond simple recall.
This is a short-answer physics derivation rather than a direct lookup or multiple-choice identification.
verifiability: Answer is a compact symbolic relation: `B_2 = \bar{B}_1 / 2`.
The expected output form is clear and can be checked exactly or by algebraic equivalence.

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: The betatron accelerates particles through the emf induced by an increasing magnetic field within the particle's orbit. Let $\bar{B}_{1}$ be the average field within the particle orbit of radius $R$, and let $B_{2}$ be the field at the orbit (see Fig. 2.80). extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object string representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: Suppose the magnetic field is oriented in the $z$ direction, i.e., $\mathbf{B}_{2}=B_{2}\,\mathrm{e}_{x}$. From $\nabla \times \mathbf{E}=-\frac{\partial \mathrm{B}}{\partial t}$, where $\frac{\partial \mathrm{B}}{\partial t}>0$, we see that the electric field is along the $-\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$ direction and has axial symmetry. Then from $$\oint_{C}\mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l}=-\int_{S}\dot{\mathbf{B}}\cdot d\mathbf{S},$$ we have $$2\pi R E=-\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \mathbf{B}_{1}\cdot d\mathbf{S}.$$ The average magnetic field is $$\bar{B}_{1}=\frac{\int \mathbf{B}_{1}\cdot d\mathbf{S}}{\pi R^{2}}.$$ Hence $$E=-\frac{R}{2}\frac{d\bar{B}_{1}}{dt}.$$ If the effect of radiation damping is negligible the equation of the motion of the particle is $$\frac{d(m\mathbf{v})}{dt}=q\mathbf{E}+q\mathbf{v}\times \mathbf{B}_{2}.$$ In cylindrical coordinates, this is equivalent to two equations: $$\begin{array}{ll}\frac{mv^{2}}{R}=qvB_{2} & \text{in }\mathbf{e}_{r}\text{ direction}\\ \frac{d(mv)}{dt}=-qE=\frac{qR}{2}\frac{d\bar{B}_{1}}{dt} & \text{in }\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\text{ direction}\end{array}$$ The last equation can be integrated to give $mv=\frac{1}{2}qR\bar{B}_{1}$ assuming $v=0$, $\bar{B}_{1}=0$ at $t=0$. Thus $B_{2}=\frac{mv}{R}$ and $\bar{B}_{1}=\frac{2mv}{R}$, so we require $B_{2}=\bar{B}_{1}/2$.

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: Suppose the magnetic field is oriented in the $z$ direction, i.e., $\mathbf{B}_{2}=B_{2}\,\mathrm{e}_{x}$. From $\nabla \times \mathbf{E}=-\frac{\partial \mathrm{B}}{\partial t}$, where $\frac{\partial \mathrm{B}}{\partial t}>0$, we see that the electric field is along the $-\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$ direction and has axial symmetry. Then from $$\oint_{C}\mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{l}=-\int_{S}\dot{\mathbf{B}}\cdot d\mathbf{S},$$ we have $$2\pi R E=-\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int \mathbf{B}_{1}\cdot d\mathbf{S}.$$ The average magnetic field is $$\bar{B}_{1}=\frac{\int \mathbf{B}_{1}\cdot d\mathbf{S}}{\pi R^{2}}.$$ Hence $$E=-\frac{R}{2}\frac{d\bar{B}_{1}}{dt}.$$ If the effect of radiation damping is negligible the equation of the motion of the particle is $$\frac{d(m\mathbf{v})}{dt}=q\mathbf{E}+q\mathbf{v}\times \mathbf{B}_{2}.$$ In cylindrical coordinates, this is equivalent to two equations: $$\begin{array}{ll}\frac{mv^{2}}{R}=qvB_{2} & \text{in }\mathbf{e}_{r}\text{ direction}\\ \frac{d(mv)}{dt}=-qE=\frac{qR}{2}\frac{d\bar{B}_{1}}{dt} & \text{in }\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\text{ direction}\end{array}$$ The last equation can be integrated to give $mv=\frac{1}{2}qR\bar{B}_{1}$ assuming $v=0$, $\bar{B}_{1}=0$ at $t=0$. Thus $B_{2}=\frac{mv}{R}$ and $\bar{B}_{1}=\frac{2mv}{R}$, so we require $B_{2}=\bar{B}_{1}/2$.
